one_line_takeaway: LLMs can tell you that C/C++ code is vulnerable and often name the CWE, but they largely cannot pinpoint the root cause or trigger line -- VulDetectBench exposes this gap with a five-task, increasing-difficulty benchmark over 17 models.
key_finding: There is a sharp capability cliff: existing LLMs handle vulnerability identification and classification reasonably (best T1 accuracy ~85%, some T2 scores >90%) but collapse on fine-grained localization, with detailed root-cause/trigger tasks generally under ~30% and often near zero. This shows that strong T1/T2 scores do NOT imply real understanding of the vulnerability mechanism -- models judge presence from high-level surface features rather than the actual bug. Larger models within a family generally do better (up to +46.9% on T1, +115.0% on T2 from scaling), and GPT-4 is the strongest overall on the hard localization tasks; Ernie 4.0 leads T1 but degrades on URS because its outputs contain much irrelevant content.
core_contribution: VulDetectBench, a benchmark that evaluates LLMs on software vulnerability detection through five tasks of progressively increasing difficulty rather than the single binary-classification framing of prior work. The five tasks are: (T1) vulnerability existence detection (binary YES/NO), (T2) CWE type inference (5-choice multi-class), (T3) key data-object and function identification, (T4) root-cause line location, and (T5) trigger-point line location. Tasks 3-5 push beyond simple 'is it vulnerable?' to whether a model can actually localize the cause and trigger inside full, multi-function C/C++ code. The authors build a curated dataset (favoring real-project data with 100%-accurate manual line-level annotations, and deliberately using only the 'no vulnerability' entries from the noisier Big-Vul/Devign sets), design task-specific prompts and metrics, and run a comprehensive evaluation of 17 LLMs (10 families, 6B-70B, incl. 3 closed-source). NOTE: static/dynamic analysis, deep-learning detectors (VulDeePecker, graph NNs), and prior LLM benchmarks are all Related Work, not this paper's own method.
threat_model: Not an attack/defense paper; it is an evaluation/benchmark for a defensive capability. The implicit use case is a security analyst using an LLM as an auxiliary tool to find, classify, and localize vulnerabilities in large C/C++ codebases (open-source libraries/kernels where memory-management bugs are common). The paper explicitly notes a dual-use societal concern: LLM-based vuln detection could be misused for malicious exploitation or lead to over-reliance, warranting responsible development.
limitations: By the authors: dataset covers only C/C++ and a limited set of vulnerability types; future work should add languages and types. Inputs restricted to <4K tokens (one vuln per sample), so long-context / multi-vulnerability behavior is untested (a longer 8k-32k set is promised but not yet evaluated here). Reviewer-noted: T3/T4/T5 rest on only 100 samples, limiting per-CWE statistical power (some CWE cells in Tables 4-5 have as few as 7-8 cases); the single traditional-tool baseline (flawfinder) is weak, so the 'LLMs vs traditional tools' comparison is thin; and the design choice to keep only 'no vulnerability' entries from Big-Vul/Devign means those noisy datasets contribute only negatives.

datasets_used: NIST SARD Juliet C/C++ 1.3.1 (synthetic, 64099 entries, label accuracy 1.000)
Devign (real, 27318 entries, label accuracy 0.800; only 'no vulnerability' samples used, for T1/T2)
Big-Vul (real, 43523 entries, label accuracy 0.543; only 'no vulnerability' samples used, for T1/T2)
quantitative_results: Task 1 (existence, ACC|F1): Ernie 4.0 best at 85.01%|86.65%; Mixtral-8x22B 81.82%|84.47% (best open-source ACC); Llama3-8B 69.40%|76.53% (best small-model F1); GPT-4 71.43%|79.30%
Task 2 (CWE type, SE|ME): GPT-4 best at 92.96|95.17; Qwen-14B 77.90|82.80; Mixtral-8x22B 77.80|74.80; Ernie 4.0 72.50|70.00. Moderate metric raises scores ~5% on average (up to +12% for Vicuna-7B)
Task 3 (key objects/functions, MAR|MIR): Mixtral-8x22B best at 30.26|24.10; Ernie 4.0 27.87|22.54; Llama3-8B 22.83|17.99; GPT-4 20.21|16.07
Task 4 (root-cause location, URS|ORS): GPT-4 best at 24.26|27.07; Mixtral-8x22B 17.49|11.46; Gemini-pro 14.64|23.51; several models (Vicuna-7B/13B, Llama2-7B, Llama3-70B) score 0|0
Task 5 (trigger-point location, URS|ORS): Ernie 4.0 22.43|10.38 and GPT-4 13.00|17.85 lead; Gemini-pro 07.56|18.89; multiple models score 0|0
Scaling within a family gives up to +46.9% on Task 1 and +115.0% on Task 2, though Vicuna-13B's F1 dropped 52.2% vs the 7B version
Per-CWE T2 accuracy: GPT-4 hits 1.00 on CWE-78/23/36/89/114/15 but only 0.62 on CWE-476 and 0.50 on CWE-191; open models lag most on rarer/procedural CWEs
Overall: >80% accuracy achievable on identification/classification tasks vs <30% on detailed localization tasks
baselines_compared: 17 LLMs across 10 families: GPT-4 (gpt4-turbo-0613), Gemini-pro, Ernie 4.0, Deepseek-coder-7B, Qwen1.5-7B/14B, ChatGLM3-6B, Vicuna-7B/13B-v1.5, CodeLlama-7B/13B, Llama3-8B/70B, Llama2-7B/13B, Mixtral-8x7B, Mixtral-8x22B

Traditional static-analysis tool flawfinder as a non-LLM baseline (completes all five tasks but produces no correct results and many false positives); Infer and CodeQL discussed but excluded because they require compilation / manually built databases
